verses.text: These are the sons of Israel; Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun, Dan, Joseph, and Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. Guidance: After the long sweep through all the nations in chapter 1, the genealogy at last arrives at its true subject: 'the sons of Israel' — the twelve patriarchs of the tribes. The Chronicler names them all, the whole covenant family, before he focuses in on the tribe that matters most for his purpose. The chapter is showing us the narrowing of the genealogy reaching its goal: from Adam, to Shem, to Abraham, to Isaac, to Israel, and now to Israel's twelve sons — the chosen people through whom the LORD's redemptive purpose runs. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's election, though it narrows to one family, embraces all twelve tribes as His covenant people; none of the sons of Israel is forgotten in the record. For the post-exilic community, many of whom could no longer trace which tribe they belonged to after the upheavals of exile, this naming of all twelve was a reaffirmation of the whole people's identity. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD keeps the full account of His people — every tribe, every family — and that to belong to 'the sons of Israel' is to belong to the covenant the LORD made and has never abandoned. Having named the twelve, the Chronicler will now turn first not to the firstborn Reuben (whose birthright was forfeited) but to Judah — the tribe of the scepter, the tribe of David and of the coming Messiah.

The sons of Judah; Er, and Onan, and Shelah: which three were born unto him of the daughter of Shua the Canaanitess. And Er, the firstborn of Judah, was evil in the sight of the LORD; and he slew him. And Tamar his daughter in law bare him Pharez and Zerah. All the sons of Judah were five. The sons of Pharez; Hezron, and Hamul. And the sons of Zerah; Zimri, and Ethan, and Heman, and Calcol, and Dara: five of them in all. And the sons of Carmi; Achar, the troubler of Israel, who transgressed in the thing accursed. And the sons of Ethan; Azariah. Guidance: The Chronicler begins the line of Judah — and immediately the genealogy becomes morally honest. Judah's first sons came from a Canaanite wife; the firstborn Er 'was evil in the sight of the LORD; and he slew him.' The line of promise actually continues through Pharez (Perez), born of Tamar — the daughter-in-law Judah wronged, in the troubling episode of Genesis 38. And the Chronicler does not hide the family's later shame either: he names 'Achar [Achan], the troubler of Israel, who transgressed in the thing accursed' (the man whose sin at Jericho brought defeat and death on Israel, Joshua 7). The chapter is showing us that the genealogy of the chosen tribe — the very line that leads to David and the Messiah — is laced with sin, scandal, and judgment. The firstborn was killed for his evil; the line continued through a union born of deception and grief; a notorious sinner stands in the record. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth about grace: the LORD's redemptive line ran not through a family of spotless saints but through real sinners, and He sovereignly carried His promise forward despite — even through — their failures. The chapter is teaching us that the Chronicler does not whitewash his people's history; the troubler of Israel is named alongside the patriarchs. And it is teaching us, for the Christian reader, the wonder that this same Judah-line, with Tamar's name attached, appears in the genealogy of Jesus Christ (Matt 1:3) — the Messiah came through a lineage marked by sin and grace, that He might be the Savior of sinners. The LORD's faithfulness, not human worthiness, is what carries the line; He writes His redemption straight through the crooked lines of human failure.

The sons also of Hezron, that were born unto him; Jerahmeel, and Ram, and Chelubai. And Ram begat Amminadab; and Amminadab begat Nahshon, prince of the children of Judah; And Nahshon begat Salma, and Salma begat Boaz, And Boaz begat Obed, and Obed begat Jesse, And Jesse begat his firstborn Eliab, and Abinadab the second, and Shimma the third, Nethaneel the fourth, Raddai the fifth, Ozem the sixth, David the seventh: Whose sisters were Zeruiah, and Abigail. And the sons of Zeruiah; Abishai, and Joab, and Asahel, three. And Abigail bare Amasa: and the father of Amasa was Jether the Ishmeelite. Guidance: Here is the heart of the whole genealogy — the line of promise running straight to David. From Hezron through Ram, the cascade descends: Amminadab, Nahshon ('prince of the children of Judah,' the leader of Judah in the wilderness, Num 1:7), Salma, Boaz (the kinsman-redeemer of Ruth), Obed, Jesse — 'and Jesse begat his firstborn Eliab... David the seventh.' All the long chapters of names, from Adam through the nations, through Israel and Judah, have been pressing toward this one name: David. The chapter is showing us the goal toward which the Chronicler's entire genealogy has been moving: the man after God's own heart, the king with whom the LORD made His everlasting covenant, the ancestor of the Messiah. David is named seventh among Jesse's sons — the number of completion — and around him the Chronicler also names his sisters Zeruiah and Abigail, and their sons (Joab, Abishai, Asahel, Amasa), the warriors who would fill David's story. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's patient work through countless generations was always aiming at the establishment of the Davidic line and, through it, the coming of the King in whom the covenant would find its eternal fulfillment. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community — who had no Davidic king on the throne — that the Davidic line was the very point of their history, traceable, intact, and bearing the unbreakable promise of 2 Samuel 7. The chapter is teaching us, in the appearance of Boaz and Obed (from the book of Ruth), that the LORD wove even a Moabite woman's faith into the royal line; grace and providence run all through this lineage. And for the Christian reader, this line — Boaz, Obed, Jesse, David — is the very genealogy of Jesus Christ (Matt 1:5-6; Luke 3:31-32). Every name in the long march was a stepping-stone toward the Son of David, the King whose throne endures forever. The whole point of the genealogy is now in view: out of all the families of the earth, the LORD was tracing the line to David, and beyond David to David's greater Son.

And Caleb the son of Hezron begat children of Azubah his wife, and of Jerioth: her sons are these; Jesher, and Shobab, and Ardon. And when Azubah was dead, Caleb took unto him Ephrath, which bare him Hur. And Hur begat Uri, and Uri begat Bezaleel. And afterward Hezron went in to the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead, whom he married when he was threescore years old; and she bare him Segub. And Segub begat Jair, who had three and twenty cities in the land of Gilead. And he took Geshur, and Aram, with the towns of Jair, from them, with Kenath, and the towns thereof, even threescore cities. All these belonged to the sons of Machir the father of Gilead. And after that Hezron was dead in Calebephratah, then Abiah Hezron's wife bare him Ashur the father of Tekoa. Guidance: Having traced the royal line to David, the Chronicler now broadens out to the wider clans of Judah descended from Hezron — beginning with Caleb son of Hezron (a Calebite ancestor, related to but distinct from the famous spy Caleb son of Jephunneh). One name in this list shines: 'Hur begat Uri, and Uri begat Bezaleel' — Bezaleel, the master craftsman whom the LORD filled with His Spirit to build the tabernacle and its furnishings (Exod 31:1-5). The chapter is showing us that the genealogy honors not only kings but craftsmen — the man whose Spirit-given skill built the place of worship is carefully placed in Judah's record. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are diverse in their callings, and that He values and records the worker of beauty for the sanctuary as surely as the warrior or the king. Bezaleel's inclusion is especially fitting in Chronicles, a book preoccupied with the temple and its worship: the genealogy reminds the post-exilic community, who were rebuilding the temple, that their own tribe of Judah had produced the first divinely-gifted craftsman of the LORD's house. The chapter is teaching us that skill and artistry, when devoted to the LORD's worship and empowered by His Spirit, are a high and honorable calling, worthy of permanent record. The wider Judahite clans — Caleb's branches, the families of Gilead through Hezron's late marriage, the towns of Jair, the father of Tekoa — fill out the tribe's territory and place in the land, the inheritance the returning exiles were reclaiming.

And the sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron were, Ram the firstborn, and Bunah, and Oren, and Ozem, and Ahijah. Jerahmeel had also another wife, whose name was Atarah; she was the mother of Onam. And the sons of Ram the firstborn of Jerahmeel were, Maaz, and Jamin, and Eker. And the sons of Onam were, Shammai, and Jada. And the sons of Shammai; Nadab, and Abishur. And the name of the wife of Abishur was Abihail, and she bare him Ahban, and Molid. And the sons of Nadab; Seled, and Appaim: but Seled died without children. And the sons of Appaim; Ishi. And the sons of Ishi; Sheshan. And the children of Sheshan; Ahlai. And the sons of Jada the brother of Shammai; Jether, and Jonathan: and Jether died without children. And the sons of Jonathan; Peleth, and Zaza. These were the sons of Jerahmeel. Guidance: The Chronicler now records the line of Jerahmeel, Hezron's firstborn, in careful detail — branch after branch of the Jerahmeelite clan of Judah. Amid the long list, small human notes appear: 'Seled died without children'; 'Jether died without children.' The chapter is showing us the Chronicler's faithfulness in recording even the families that ended, the branches that bore no fruit, the men who died childless. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's record of His people is comprehensive and tender — it includes not only the flourishing lines that produced kings and craftsmen but also the quiet households, the lesser clans, and even the lines that died out. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community that every family of Judah had its place in the people of God, however obscure. In a society where genealogy determined one's inheritance, one's tribal belonging, and (for some) one's eligibility for priestly or other service, these careful records were precious — they told a returning family who they were and where they belonged. The chapter is teaching us that no member of the covenant people is beneath the LORD's notice; He numbers and names the small clans and the childless alongside the great. The Jerahmeelite line, dwelling in the Negev south of Judah, was part of the covenant people, and the Chronicler honors it with the same care he gave to the royal line.

Now Sheshan had no sons, but daughters. And Sheshan had a servant, an Egyptian, whose name was Jarha. And Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha his servant to wife; and she bare him Attai. And Attai begat Nathan, and Nathan begat Zabad, And Zabad begat Ephlal, and Ephlal begat Obed, And Obed begat Jehu, and Jehu begat Azariah, And Azariah begat Helez, and Helez begat Eleasah, And Eleasah begat Sisamai, and Sisamai begat Shallum, And Shallum begat Jekamiah, and Jekamiah begat Elishama. Guidance: A remarkable little story is embedded in the genealogy. Sheshan 'had no sons, but daughters,' and so he gave his daughter in marriage to his own Egyptian servant Jarha — and through that union the family line continued for many generations (Attai, Nathan, Zabad, and on down to Elishama). The chapter is showing us a striking instance of grace and inclusion: an Egyptian slave is grafted into the covenant people of Judah by marriage, and his descendants are honored in the sacred genealogy. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people were never a closed, racially-pure club; from the beginning, foreigners who joined themselves to Israel — like Jarha the Egyptian, like Ruth the Moabitess in the royal line, like Rahab — were received and woven into the family of God. The chapter is teaching us the breadth of the covenant: the LORD makes a place among His people for the outsider who is joined to them. The chapter is teaching us, too, the LORD's provision for the family with no male heir: when Sheshan had only daughters, the line did not perish; the LORD provided continuance through a son-in-law, even one of foreign and servile origin. For the post-exilic community wrestling with questions of who belonged and who did not, this quiet record of an Egyptian servant honored in Judah's genealogy testified that faith and covenant-belonging, not bloodline alone, marked the people of God — a truth that opens toward the gospel, in which 'there is neither Greek nor Jew' but all who are Christ's are Abraham's seed (Gal 3:28-29).

For the post-exilic community wrestling with questions of who belonged and who did not, this quiet record of an Egyptian servant honored in Judah's genealogy testified that faith and covenant-belonging, not bloodline alone, marked the people of God — a truth that opens toward the gospel, in which 'there is neither Greek nor Jew' but all who are Christ's are Abraham's seed (Gal 3:28-29).

Now the sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel were, Mesha his firstborn, which was the father of Ziph; and the sons of Mareshah the father of Hebron. And the sons of Hebron; Korah, and Tappuah, and Rekem, and Shema. And Shema begat Raham, the father of Jorkoam: and Rekem begat Shammai. And the son of Shammai was Maon: and Maon was the father of Bethzur. And Ephah, Caleb's concubine, bare Haran, and Moza, and Gazez: and Haran begat Gazez. And the sons of Jahdai; Regem, and Jotham, and Geshan, and Pelet, and Ephah, and Shaaph. Maachah, Caleb's concubine, bare Sheber, and Tirhanah. She bare also Shaaph the father of Madmannah, Sheva the father of Machbenah, and the father of Gibea: and the daughter of Caleb was Achsah. Guidance: The Chronicler returns to the line of Caleb (here 'the brother of Jerahmeel'), and the names that follow are largely place-names personified — Ziph, Hebron, Tappuah, Maon, Beth-zur, Madmannah — the towns and regions of the southern Judean hill country and the Negev. The genealogy here functions almost as a map: it records which clans of Judah settled which towns, anchoring families to the territory they held in the land. The chapter is showing us that genealogy and geography are intertwined in Israel — these names tie the families of Judah to their towns, their inheritance, their place in the promised land. The chapter is teaching us how precious land and place were to the covenant people: the LORD had given Israel the land as an inheritance, and these records preserved the memory of which family belonged where. For the returned exiles, re-settling the towns of Judah and Benjamin around Jerusalem, such records helped re-establish their rightful claims to the ancestral land the LORD had given. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is a God of place as well as people — He settled His people in a specific land, recorded their towns, and these genealogies bound the families of Judah to the soil of promise. The mention of Hebron (the city of the patriarchs' tombs, David's first capital) and these southern towns roots the tribe of Judah deep in the land of promise. Even the daughters are remembered — 'the daughter of Caleb was Achsah,' the woman who boldly asked her father for springs of water (Josh 15:16-19) — another sign of the Chronicler's care to record all the family, sons and daughters alike.

These were the sons of Caleb the son of Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah; Shobal the father of Kirjathjearim, Salma the father of Bethlehem, Hareph the father of Bethgader. And Shobal the father of Kirjathjearim had sons; Haroeh, and half of the Manahethites. And the families of Kirjathjearim; the Ithrites, and the Puhites, and the Shumathites, and the Mishraites; of them came the Zareathites, and the Eshtaulites. The sons of Salma; Bethlehem, and the Netophathites, Ataroth, the house of Joab, and half of the Manahethites, the Zorites. And the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez; the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, and Suchathites. These are the Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab. Guidance: The chapter closes with the families descended from Caleb son of Hur, and one name leaps out for any reader who knows the gospel: 'Salma the father of Bethlehem.' Bethlehem — the little town that would be the birthplace of David, and a thousand years later the birthplace of David's greater Son, the Messiah (Micah 5:2; Matt 2:1). The genealogy records the founding clans of Bethlehem, Kirjath-jearim (where the ark would rest for twenty years), and other Judean towns. It also notes 'the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez' and 'the Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab' — the Rechabites, that austere, faithful clan whose loyalty Jeremiah would one day hold up as an example (Jer 35), here shown to be grafted into Judah through the Kenites. The chapter is showing us, in its closing names, the quiet seeds of enormous future significance: the town of Bethlehem founded; the faithful Rechabites woven into the people; the scribal families who would preserve the Scriptures. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD plants, in obscure genealogical notes, the beginnings of His greatest works — the town where the Bread of Life would be born is here recorded as just one more Judean settlement, founded by Salma. The chapter is teaching us to read even the driest lists with expectant eyes, for the LORD hides the future in the present, the great in the small. The whole genealogy of Judah, with David at its center and Bethlehem at its edge, is the LORD's long preparation for the coming of the King. The post-exilic community, reading these names, were being reminded that they belonged to the tribe of promise, settled in the towns of promise, awaiting the fulfillment of the promise — the Son of David, born in Bethlehem of Judah, who would reign forever. The genealogy is not a dead list but a living hope, tracing the line and the place from which salvation would come.

The sons of Salma; Bethlehem, and the Netophathites, Ataroth, the house of Joab, and half of the Manahethites, the Zorites. And the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez; the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, and Suchathites. These are the Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab. Guidance: The chapter closes with the families descended from Caleb son of Hur, and one name leaps out for any reader who knows the gospel: 'Salma the father of Bethlehem.' Bethlehem — the little town that would be the birthplace of David, and a thousand years later the birthplace of David's greater Son, the Messiah (Micah 5:2; Matt 2:1). The genealogy records the founding clans of Bethlehem, Kirjath-jearim (where the ark would rest for twenty years), and other Judean towns. It also notes 'the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez' and 'the Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab' — the Rechabites, that austere, faithful clan whose loyalty Jeremiah would one day hold up as an example (Jer 35), here shown to be grafted into Judah through the Kenites. The chapter is showing us, in its closing names, the quiet seeds of enormous future significance: the town of Bethlehem founded; the faithful Rechabites woven into the people; the scribal families who would preserve the Scriptures. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD plants, in obscure genealogical notes, the beginnings of His greatest works — the town where the Bread of Life would be born is here recorded as just one more Judean settlement, founded by Salma. The chapter is teaching us to read even the driest lists with expectant eyes, for the LORD hides the future in the present, the great in the small. The whole genealogy of Judah, with David at its center and Bethlehem at its edge, is the LORD's long preparation for the coming of the King. The post-exilic community, reading these names, were being reminded that they belonged to the tribe of promise, settled in the towns of promise, awaiting the fulfillment of the promise — the Son of David, born in Bethlehem of Judah, who would reign forever. The genealogy is not a dead list but a living hope, tracing the line and the place from which salvation would come.
